prompt: Why do software engineering agents need post-training that is grounded in tools and executable feedback?
reference_answer: Software engineering agents need post-training grounded in tools and executable feedback because issue-resolution tasks can be verified with repository state and tests instead of only free-form judgments. SWE-RL demonstrates the value of reinforcement learning on software engineering tasks, while SWE-Master layers agentic SFT, RL, test-time scaling, and LSP or code tools into a stronger system. The broader lesson is that SWE agents need environment feedback and tool use, not just answer-only optimization.

documents.title: SWE-RL digest
documents.content: SWE-RL applies reinforcement learning to software engineering agents on issue-resolution tasks. The key intuition is that repository state and executable tests provide richer feedback than answer-only judging.

documents.title: SWE-Master repository digest
documents.content: SWE-Master combines agentic supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, test-time scaling, and tool-augmented coding workflows including LSP-style support. The system treats post-training as an integrated stack rather than a single optimization trick, so tools and post-training are optimized together.

documents.title: SWE-bench digest
documents.content: SWE-bench evaluates issue resolution against real repositories and executable outcomes, giving software engineering agents verifiable environment feedback on realistic coding tasks. This makes software engineering a setting where tools and environment feedback matter directly.

required_claims.text: Software engineering agents benefit from executable or repository-grounded feedback rather than answer-only supervision.
SWE-Master extends post-training by combining agentic SFT, RL, test-time scaling, and tool-augmented coding support.
The broader reason SWE agents need post-training is that realistic coding tasks require tool use plus verifiable environment feedback.

provenance.refresh_hint: Refresh if SWE post-training stacks or benchmark semantics change materially upstream.
notes: Focused on executable-feedback and tool-grounded software engineering agents.
